topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The hammer felt impossibly heavy in my grip. Every time I lifted it, my knuckles went white, and a cold sweat immediately prickled the back of my neck. Mr. Henderson was standing on his lawn, beaming, gesturing expansively towards the patch of overgrown shrubbery that bordered our property line. "Just a little boundary definition, dear neighbor," he'd chirped, his voice unnervingly cheerful. My breath hitched in my throat. I stared at the splintered wood in my hand, the rhythmic thudding against the metal siding of my mind sounding like a frantic drum solo. What if I misjudged the posts? What if the whole structure leaned precariously? My vision tunneled, focusing only on the gap between the fence post and the ground, calculating the trajectory of disaster before I even swung the tool.

[story 2]

"So, you're ready to start the work?" Mrs. Gable asked, her tone light, almost breezy. I kept my hands clasped so tightly my fingers ached, refusing to look directly at the pile of lumber. The sun felt too bright, exposing every tremor in my hands. The sheer scale of the task—the need to measure, to drive stakes into the earth, to secure this barrier—was overwhelming. My mouth went dry. I took a shallow, quick breath, trying to steady the tremor running through my legs. If I dropped the level, if the wire didn't catch right, the entire endeavor would collapse into a mess. I wanted to bolt, to retreat to the safety of my own house, but I was rooted to the spot, watching Mr. Henderson’s expectant face, feeling a tightness constricting my chest until it was hard to draw a full breath.

[story 3]

I kept repeating the measurements in my head, over and over, trying to force order into the chaos swirling inside my skull. The wood smelled sharp, the soil beneath my boots felt spongy and unstable. Each decision felt monumental, a single misstep capable of unraveling everything. When Mr. Henderson pointed towards the property line, the world seemed to narrow down to that single, dividing line. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out the sounds of the neighborhood. I fumbled with the nails, dropping a few onto the grass with a sharp clatter. A wave of sheer terror washed over me; this wasn't just about a fence; it felt like an imminent, catastrophic failure of my ability to manage anything. I squeezed my eyes shut for a second, trying to force the shaking out of my hands.
